title: On the potential for high-accuracy spectroscopy of $\mathrm{H}_2^+$ and $\overline{\mathrm{H}}_2^-$ in Penning traps for a test of CPT invariance
authors: Stephan Schiller, Juan M. Cornejo, Nikita Poljakov, Christian Ospelkaus, Stefan Ulmer, Dimitar Bakalov
abstract: The comparison of vibrational transition frequencies of $\mathrm{H}_2^+$ and $\overline{\mathrm{H}}_2^-$ offers a new opportunity to test CPT invariance. Myers [Phys. Rev. A 98, 010101(R) (2018)] proposed performing laser spectroscopy in a Penning trap (PT) with non-destructive read-out. Here, we provide an extensive analysis of this proposal, introduce novel aspects, and discuss its implementation in PTs that incorporate either the continuous Stern-Gerlach effect or quantum-logic spectroscopy. We derive estimates for the achievable accuracy of the test. We find that a comparison of the vibrational frequencies at a fractional level of $1\times10^{-17}$ is a realistic prospect, using technology that is mostly already available. We also analyze complementary CPT invariance tests, namely those of the g-factor of the bound electron/positron via electron-spin-resonance spectroscopy and of the magnetic moment of the proton/antiproton via radiofrequency spectroscopy.
summary: This paper investigates the potential for testing CPT invariance by comparing the vibrational spectra of hydrogen molecular ions and their antimatter counterparts in Penning traps. It identifies optimal spectroscopic transitions and provides a roadmap for achieving fractional uncertainties as low as 10^-17. The work highlights how managing magnetic and electric field-induced shifts is crucial for the success of such fundamental symmetry tests.
main_problem: Evaluating the feasibility of using high-accuracy spectroscopy of H2+ and anti-H2- in Penning traps to perform tests of CPT invariance.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that comparing vibrational frequencies at a fractional level of 1e-17 is a realistic prospect using existing technology, provided specific transitions and trap parameters are optimized to mitigate systematic shifts.
method: The study uses an extensive theoretical analysis of spectroscopic transitions, including the derivation of systematic shifts (Zeeman, Stark, Doppler) and the evaluation of different detection regimes like continuous Stern-Gerlach effect and quantum-logic spectroscopy.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of molecular hydrogen ions (H2+) and their antimatter counterparts (anti-H2-) trapped in cryogenic Penning traps under strong magnetic fields (e.g., 4 T). The model utilizes an effective Hamiltonian including hyperfine, Zeeman, rotational, and diamagnetic interactions.
key_observables: Vibrational transition frequencies, electron-spin-flip frequency, magnetic moments of proton/antiproton, and frequency differences between matter and antimatter species.
important_parameters: Magnetic field strength (B ~ 4 T), fractional uncertainty target (1e-17 to 1e-18), axial and radial temperatures, and vibrational/rotational quantum numbers.
main_assumptions: Assumes the eventual availability of anti-H2- for study and that magnetic field stability and trap parameters (like the magnetic bottle coefficient) can be controlled with extreme precision.
figures_summary: The notes mention a schematic of spin state energies and predicted spectroscopic line shapes under different quadrupole Doppler shift regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the CPT test proposal, provides a detailed Hamiltonian analysis of the molecular ions, evaluates various systematic frequency shifts, discusses experimental detection strategies (CSGE and QLS), and analyzes methods for mitigating Doppler and Stark shifts.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant for AMO physicists working on precision spectroscopy and antimatter research, as it defines the fundamental limits of frequency metrology using molecular ions and proposes new methods for controlling systematic errors in high-precision traps.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1.: Schematic of the spin structure of two selected rovibrational energy levels of para-H+ 2 in a B0 = 4 Tesla magnetic field and selected transitions. Ms, M ′ s for electron-spin projection and MN, M ′ N for rotational-angular-momentum-projection are approximate quantum numbers, used here to label the states. Cyan: Two transitions considered to be suitable for a CPTI test, i.e. having small systematic shifts, are indicated. These transitions also appear in figure 5 as A, A′. fvib,0 is the spin-averaged rovibrational transition frequency in zero B-field, i.e. the hypothetical value if Htot = 0 (Htot includes the spin-rotation interaction.) The indicated offsets with respect to fvib,0...

Figure 2.: Sensitivities β (full lines) and magnetic-field shifts ∆fmag (dashed lines) of the (v = 0, N, Ms, MN = 0) →(v′ = 2, N ′ = 2, M ′ s = Ms, M ′ N = 0) transitions as a function of magnetic field B. The cases of lower rotational quantum number N = 0 and N = 2 are shown.

Figure 3.: Example of ion preparation in a PT. The trap is loaded with particles; afterwards the resonant center of the axial detector spectrum is observed with a certain bandwidth, while a weak axial resonant drive is applied and the trap voltage is being ramped to bring different ion species to resonance with the detector. In between the upper and the lower spectrum resonant axial cleaning drives are applied, to remove particles from the trap. The lower spectrum is recorded after cleaning all particles out, except for a cloud of protons. Selecting the resonant cleaning drive frequencies differently, any particle species can be prepared.

Figure 4.: Proposed multi-Penning-trap stack for precision spectroscopy on H+ 2 and H − 2 . The stack consists of seven traps. In the center is a highly shielded and homogenized precision trap with radial and axial laser access. Moving outwards, analysis traps for CSGE detection and cooling traps for sub-thermal cooling are located. The outer traps are reservoir traps. All traps are equipped with resonant detection systems for pick-up of axial and cyclotron image currents and resistive cooling (not shown for the left traps in order to keep the figure organized).

Figure 5.: The CSGE procedure for nondestructive detection and spectroscopy of a rovibrational transition. The concept is from Myers [21]. For clarity, only the minimum number of states required is shown. The figure shows the case where in step 1 it was found that the molecule is in the lower spin state of (v, N). Then step 2 (vibrational excitation A) is taken, followed by step 3 (detection). If no spin flip in (v′, N ′) is detected (by CSGE), step 2 is repeated. This continues until a spin flip is detected. Then, the “downward” excitation A′, step 4, is attempted. The following steps are analogous to those connected with the upward excitation. Note that fA ̸= fA′; example values are...
